reviews.titleZh: Chapter 1 — EMS Systems, Roles, and Responsibilities
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 1 — EMS Systems, Roles, and Responsibilities
reviews.summaryZh: 这章先定义 EMS 系统怎么运作、paramedic 在系统中的定位，以及 profession、scope、medical direction 和 quality improvement 的基本框架。它本质上是在回答：你在 EMS 体系里是谁、你能做什么、你为什么要对自己的行为负责。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter defines how EMS systems function and where the paramedic fits within the system, including professionalism, scope, medical direction, and quality improvement. At its core, it answers: who you are in the EMS system, what you are allowed to do, and why you are accountable for your actions.
highlightsZh: 搞清 EMS system 的组成
理解 paramedic 的角色与责任
知道 scope、accountability 和 medical direction 的边界
highlightsEn: Understand EMS system structure
Define the paramedic role and responsibilities
Know the boundaries of scope, accountability, and medical direction
keyPointsZh: EMS 不是单一救护车，而是一个由 dispatch、现场救援、转运、接收系统、medical oversight 和 quality improvement 组成的完整系统。
paramedic / EMR 的角色不只是“把病人送到医院”，而是要在现场 assessment、initial management、decision-making 和 handoff 中承担专业责任。
scope of practice 决定你能做什么，但 scope 不是“你会什么”，而是“你被授权做什么”。
medical direction 是院前 care 的核心支持系统之一，说明你不是完全独立在做事，而是在一个受监管、受指导的专业体系中工作。
professionalism 不只是态度问题，它会影响病人信任、团队合作、法律风险和系统声誉。
quality improvement 说明 EMS 不是只做任务，而是要持续回顾系统表现、错误和改进空间。
keyPointsEn: EMS is not a single ambulance unit but a full system involving dispatch, scene care, transport, receiving systems, medical oversight, and quality improvement.
The paramedic/EMR role is not just to bring a patient to hospital, but to take professional responsibility for assessment, initial management, decision-making, and handoff.
Scope of practice defines what you are authorized to do; it is not simply what you personally know how to do.
Medical direction is a core support structure in prehospital care, showing that field providers work within a supervised and regulated professional system.
Professionalism is not just about attitude; it affects patient trust, teamwork, legal exposure, and system reputation.
Quality improvement means EMS is not just task performance—it is continuous review, learning, and system refinement.
mustKnowZh: 你必须分清 education / certification / licensure / registration 这些概念，它们都和“能不能执业、在什么边界下执业”有关。
你要理解 roles and responsibilities 不只是工作内容列表，而是 accountability 的来源。
medical direction、documentation、communication 和 system quality 其实从第一章就已经开始了，不是后面才出现的独立话题。
mustKnowEn: You must distinguish concepts such as education, certification, licensure, and registration, because all relate to whether and how you are allowed to practice.
Roles and responsibilities are not just a task list—they are a source of accountability.
Medical direction, documentation, communication, and system quality begin in Chapter 1 conceptually; they are not isolated topics that appear later by accident.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 scope of practice 理解成“我会做所以我能做”。实际上，scope 更接近“系统授权你做什么”。
常见误区 2：把 professionalism 当成软性内容。实际上它会直接影响病人安全、团队协作和法律责任。
常见误区 3：以为 quality improvement 是管理层的事。其实 frontline provider 也是 QI 的组成部分，因为你的记录、判断和执行都会进入系统回顾。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating scope of practice as ‘if I know how to do it, I can do it.’ In reality, scope is closer to what the system authorizes you to do.
Common confusion 2: viewing professionalism as soft content. In reality, it directly affects patient safety, teamwork, and legal responsibility.

Common confusion 3: thinking quality improvement belongs only to management. In fact, frontline providers are part of QI because their documentation, decisions, and performance feed back into system review.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 2 — The Well-Being of the Paramedic
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 2 — The Well-Being of the Paramedic
reviews.summaryZh: 这章聚焦急救人员如何长期稳定工作，包括 stress、death and dying、自我保护和职业健康。它真正处理的是：如果你自己先垮掉，后面所有 clinical skill 都没有办法长期稳定发挥。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter focuses on how paramedics stay healthy and functional over time, including stress, death and dying, self-protection, and occupational health. At a deeper level, it asks: if you break down first, none of your clinical skills can be sustained over time.
highlightsZh: 识别职业压力来源
理解 burnout 和 self-protection
知道 emergency vehicle operations 也属于个人安全议题
highlightsEn: Recognize sources of occupational stress
Understand burnout and self-protection
Know that emergency vehicle operations are also personal-safety issues
keyPointsZh: paramedic well-being 不是附属话题，而是职业能力的一部分。
高压、睡眠不足、反复暴露于死亡和创伤，会长期影响判断、反应速度和情绪稳定。
well-being 包含心理、身体、职业和社交层面，不只是“有没有 burnout”。
death and dying 是 EMS 工作的常态风险源之一，不能等真正崩掉后才处理。
self-protection 既包括 PPE 和现场安全，也包括长期压力管理、休息和界限感。
emergency vehicle operations 不只是驾驶技术问题，它本质上也是安全与职业生存问题。
keyPointsEn: Paramedic well-being is not a side topic; it is part of professional capability.
High stress, sleep loss, and repeated exposure to death and trauma can affect judgment, reaction time, and emotional stability over time.
Well-being includes psychological, physical, occupational, and social dimensions—it is more than just burnout.
Death and dying are routine EMS stressors and should be addressed before they become overwhelming.
Self-protection includes PPE and scene safety, but also long-term stress management, rest, and personal boundaries.
Emergency vehicle operations are not just driving issues—they are also safety and professional-survival issues.
mustKnowZh: 你要把 stress management 当成 clinical reliability 的组成部分，而不是“软技能”。
面对死亡、哀伤和 repeated trauma exposure 时，早识别、早处理，比硬撑更专业。
个人安全不是自私，而是 EMS 能不能持续救人的前提。
mustKnowEn: You should treat stress management as part of clinical reliability, not as a ‘soft skill.’
With death, grief, and repeated trauma exposure, early recognition and early response are more professional than simply pushing through.
Personal safety is not selfish—it is a prerequisite for EMS to keep helping others.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 well-being 理解成心理健康附加题。其实它会直接影响现场判断和 patient safety。
常见误区 2：认为 burnout 只是“太累了”。实际上 burnout 常常会先表现为冷漠、错误增多、沟通下降和风险容忍度变化。
常见误区 3：觉得 emergency vehicle safety 只是驾驶章节内容。其实它本身就是 paramedic survival 的一部分。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating well-being as an optional mental-health add-on. In reality, it directly affects field judgment and patient safety.
Common confusion 2: thinking burnout is just ‘feeling tired.’ In reality, burnout often shows up first as detachment, increased mistakes, worse communication, and changes in risk tolerance.
Common confusion 3: assuming emergency vehicle safety belongs only in a driving chapter. It is actually part of paramedic survival and sustained readiness.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章把 EMS 从事后反应拉向前端 prevention，强调 paramedic 也参与社区健康和 injury prevention。它的核心是在提醒你：真正高水平的 EMS，不只是会处理后果，也要理解如何减少后果发生。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter shifts EMS from purely reactive care toward prevention, showing that paramedics also contribute to community health and injury prevention. Its deeper message is that high-level EMS is not only about handling consequences, but also about understanding how to reduce the chance that those consequences happen at all.
highlightsZh: 理解 prevention 的公共健康价值
认识 EMS 不只是 transport
把社区教育也看成 EMS 角色的一部分
highlightsEn: Understand the public-health value of prevention
Recognize EMS is more than transport
See community education as part of the EMS role
keyPointsZh: EMS 的价值不只在事故后反应，也在于参与 injury 和 illness prevention。
prevention 不是“额外工作”，而是从系统角度减少病人进入 emergency pathway 的方式。
paramedic 在社区中不只是 responder，也可能是 educator、advocate 和 public health participant。
许多可预防伤病背后都有行为、环境和教育因素。
prevention 思维会改变你对 EMS role 的理解：不是只会运送，而是参与系统改善。
keyPointsEn: EMS creates value not only by responding after injury or illness, but also by participating in prevention.
Prevention is not ‘extra work’; it is a systems-level way to reduce how often patients enter the emergency pathway.
In the community, the paramedic may function not only as a responder but also as an educator, advocate, and public-health participant.
Many preventable injuries and illnesses are linked to behavior, environment, and education.
A prevention mindset changes how you understand the EMS role: not just transport, but system improvement.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 prevention 是 EMS identity 的一部分，而不是可有可无的附属项。
要理解公共健康视角会改变你看 injury / illness 的方式。
要记住许多 EMS 接触点，本身就是 prevention opportunity。
mustKnowEn: Know that prevention is part of EMS identity, not an optional side topic.
Understand that a public-health perspective changes how you think about injury and illness.
Remember that many EMS contact points are themselves prevention opportunities.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 prevention 是社区机构的事，不是 EMS 的事。实际上 EMS 也在 prevention 链条里。
常见误区 2：把 prevention 理解成“讲几句安全建议”。其实它还涉及系统设计、资源连接和风险识别。
常见误区 3：认为 EMS 只有在 emergency 中才有价值。Chapter 3 在打破这个想法。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking prevention belongs only to community agencies and not EMS. In reality, EMS is part of the prevention chain.
Common confusion 2: reducing prevention to simply giving safety advice. In reality, it also involves system design, resource linkage, and risk recognition.
Common confusion 3: believing EMS has value only during emergencies. Chapter 3 challenges that idea.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲法律责任、过失、病患关系、documentation 和 reportable cases，帮助建立 legal boundary。它真正回答的是：你不只是要把事做完，还要在法律上站得住。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers legal accountability, negligence, the patient relationship, documentation, and reportable cases to establish legal boundaries. At its core, it asks not only whether the job gets done, but whether it stands up legally.
highlightsZh: 搞清 negligence 和 accountability
知道 documentation 的法律价值
理解现场责任与 reportable cases
highlightsEn: Clarify negligence and accountability
Know the legal value of documentation
Understand scene responsibilities and reportable cases
keyPointsZh: 法律问题不是后置思考，而是 clinical action 从一开始就附带的边界。
paramedic–patient relationship 一旦建立，就意味着 duty、responsibility 和 accountability。
negligence 不是“我不是故意的”就没问题，而是要看是否达到合理专业标准。
documentation 不只是记录你做了什么，也是法律上说明你为什么这么做的重要证据。
crime scene responsibility 和 reportable cases 说明 EMS 不只是医学角色，也承担法定责任。
licensure / certification / regulation 不是行政细节，而是执业合法性的基础。
keyPointsEn: Legal issues are not an afterthought; they shape the boundaries of clinical action from the very beginning.
Once the paramedic–patient relationship exists, duty, responsibility, and accountability follow.
Negligence is not avoided just because harm was unintentional; the question is whether the standard of care was met.
Documentation is not only a record of what you did, but also evidence of why you did it.
Crime scene responsibility and reportable cases show that EMS has legal duties beyond pure clinical care.
Licensure, certification, and regulation are not administrative trivia—they are the foundation of lawful practice.
mustKnowZh: 要把 documentation 当成法律工具，而不只是工作日志。
要理解 negligence 和 accountability 常常和你有没有按标准做、有没有清楚记录有关。
要知道 EMS 现场行为可能同时产生医疗后果和法律后果。
mustKnowEn: Treat documentation as a legal tool, not just a work log.
Understand that negligence and accountability often relate to whether you met standards and documented clearly.
Know that EMS scene actions may create both medical and legal consequences.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得只要出发点是好的，法律上就没问题。实际上标准、过程和记录同样重要。
常见误区 2：把 documentation 当成最后补一下的文书工作。其实它常是你最重要的防线之一。
常见误区 3：以为 legal issue 只在上法庭时才 relevant。其实它从你接触病人的第一刻就已经开始。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking that good intentions automatically protect you legally. In reality, standards, process, and records matter too.
Common confusion 2: treating documentation as something to fill in at the end. In reality, it is often one of your strongest protections.
Common confusion 3: believing legal issues matter only in court. In reality, they begin the moment you make patient contact.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章处理 clinical grey zone，重点在 patient autonomy、resuscitation 决策、integrity 和专业伦理框架。它真正训练的是：不是每个难题都能用法律条文直接解决，你还需要价值判断。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter deals with clinical gray zones, focusing on patient autonomy, resuscitation decisions, integrity, and professional ethical frameworks. Its deeper lesson is that not every hard problem can be solved by a legal rule alone—you also need value-based judgment.
highlightsZh: 学会区分 legal vs ethical
理解 patient autonomy
建立灰区判断框架
highlightsEn: Differentiate legal vs ethical issues
Understand patient autonomy
Build a framework for gray-zone decisions
keyPointsZh: ethics 不是跟法律重复，而是在法律之外处理“应不应该”的问题。
patient autonomy 是院前急救中的核心伦理原则之一。
withholding / withdrawing resuscitation 不只是 protocol 问题，也牵涉价值判断和专业边界。
professional integrity 不是抽象道德感，而是日常 decision-making 的一部分。
有些 clinical situation 没有完美答案，所以要靠 ethical framework 做更稳的判断。
keyPointsEn: Ethics does not simply repeat law; it addresses whether something should be done, not just whether it can be done.
Patient autonomy is one of the central ethical principles in prehospital care.
Withholding or withdrawing resuscitation is not only a protocol issue, but also a matter of values and professional boundaries.
Professional integrity is not abstract morality; it is part of everyday clinical decision-making.
Some clinical situations have no perfect answer, so an ethical framework helps produce more stable judgment.
mustKnowZh: 要学会分开 legal answer 和 ethical answer，它们可能重叠，但不总是同一个东西。
要理解 autonomy 和 beneficence 常常会互相拉扯。
要知道伦理题不是背标准答案，而是背判断框架。
mustKnowEn: Learn to separate the legal answer from the ethical answer—they may overlap, but they are not always the same thing.
Understand that autonomy and beneficence can pull in different directions.
Know that ethics is not about memorizing perfect answers, but about learning a judgment framework.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 legal = ethical。实际上合法不一定等于最合适，反之亦然。
常见误区 2：觉得伦理题太主观，所以不用认真学。其实它在高风险灰区里非常关键。
常见误区 3：认为 patient autonomy 就等于病人说什么都照做。实际上 autonomy 也要放在 capacity、context 和专业责任里理解。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: assuming legal = ethical. In reality, what is lawful is not always what is most appropriate, and vice versa.
Common confusion 2: dismissing ethics as too subjective to study seriously. In fact, it matters most in high-risk gray zones.

Common confusion 3: assuming patient autonomy means simply doing whatever the patient says. In reality, autonomy must be understood in the context of capacity, circumstances, and professional responsibility.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章是后面所有 disease process 的底层科学，核心在于解释病人为什么会变成现在这样。它不是让你背生化，而是让你能把 shock、inflammation、acid-base imbalance、cell injury 和临床表现连起来。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter is the scientific foundation for later disease processes. Its purpose is not to make you memorize biochemistry, but to help you connect shock, inflammation, acid-base imbalance, cellular injury, and the patient’s clinical presentation.
highlightsZh: 理解 cell / tissue level 的病理变化
把 acid-base、hypoperfusion、inflammation 和 disease process 连起来
为后面所有 medical emergency chapters 打底
highlightsEn: Understand pathology at the cell and tissue level
Link acid-base balance, hypoperfusion, inflammation, and disease processes
Build the foundation for all later medical-emergency chapters
keyPointsZh: Pathophysiology 的核心不是名词，而是机制：病人为什么会恶化、为什么会代偿、为什么会失代偿。
低灌注（hypoperfusion）是后面很多急症的共同主线，不只是 shock 章节才会用到。
acid-base balance 会影响细胞功能、器官表现和病人整体稳定度。
cell injury 和 inflammation 是许多疾病过程的共同语言。
如果你不理解底层 pathophysiology，后面的症状、体征和 treatment 会变成死记硬背。
keyPointsEn: The core of pathophysiology is not terminology but mechanism: why the patient worsens, why they compensate, and why they decompensate.
Hypoperfusion is a shared pathway across many emergencies, not just the shock chapter.
Acid-base balance affects cellular function, organ behavior, and overall patient stability.
Cell injury and inflammation are common languages across many disease processes.
Without pathophysiology, later signs, symptoms, and treatments become memorization instead of understanding.
mustKnowZh: 要把“症状”往回追到机制，而不是只背表面表现。
要知道很多 emergency 最终都会回到 perfusion、oxygen delivery、cell injury 这些底层问题。
要把这章当成整个 medical 部分的“解释器”。
mustKnowEn: Trace symptoms back to mechanism rather than memorizing surface presentation only.
Know that many emergencies ultimately return to perfusion, oxygen delivery, and cellular injury.
Treat this chapter as the “interpreter” for the entire medical section.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 pathophysiology 当成只为了考试的理论章。实际上它决定你后面对病人的理解深度。
常见误区 2：觉得 acid-base、inflammation 这些太抽象。其实它们会直接体现在呼吸、循环和意识变化里。
常见误区 3：把 shock 只理解成血压低。实际上更早期、更底层的问题是 perfusion failure。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating pathophysiology as a theory chapter only for exams. In reality, it determines how deeply you understand patients later.
Common confusion 2: thinking acid-base balance and inflammation are too abstract. In reality, they show up directly in breathing, circulation, and mental-status changes.
Common confusion 3: reducing shock to low blood pressure. The earlier and deeper issue is perfusion failure.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章建立药理底盘：药物命名、来源、分类、给药途径、pharmacokinetics、pharmacodynamics 和 medication safety。它真正回答的是：药为什么有效、什么时候危险、以及为什么同一种药对不同病人反应会不同。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter builds the pharmacology foundation: medication names, sources, classification, routes, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and medication safety. At its core, it explains why drugs work, when they become dangerous, and why the same drug may affect different patients differently.
highlightsZh: 分清 pharmacokinetics 和 pharmacodynamics
理解 route、dose、classification 和 side effects 的逻辑
给后面所有 medication administration 打底
highlightsEn: Differentiate pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics
Understand the logic of route, dose, classification, and side effects
Build the foundation for all later medication administration
keyPointsZh: Pharmacology 不是背药名，而是理解药物和病人之间的关系。
pharmacokinetics 关注机体如何处理药物；pharmacodynamics 关注药物如何作用于机体。
给药途径会改变起效速度、吸收方式和风险。
side effects、contraindications 和 interactions 不是附属信息，而是安全给药的核心。
同一种药在不同病人身上可能效果不同，因为年龄、体重、器官功能和合并用药都会改变药物表现。
keyPointsEn: Pharmacology is not just memorizing drug names; it is understanding the relationship between the drug and the patient.
Pharmacokinetics explains how the body handles the drug; pharmacodynamics explains how the drug acts on the body.
Route of administration changes onset, absorption, and risk.
Side effects, contraindications, and interactions are not extra details—they are central to safe medication use.
The same drug may act differently in different patients because age, weight, organ function, and concurrent medications all matter.
mustKnowZh: 要能分清药效学和药动学。
要知道“对症”不等于“安全”，给药永远是 benefit-risk balance。
后面的每个药物题，本质都建立在这一章。
mustKnowEn: You must be able to distinguish pharmacodynamics from pharmacokinetics.
“Indicated” does not automatically mean “safe”; medication use is always a benefit-risk balance.
Every later medication question is built on this chapter.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把药理学理解成背清单。实际上重点是机制和安全。
常见误区 2：觉得 side effects 是次要信息。其实副作用常常决定你敢不敢给药。
常见误区 3：认为会给药就够了。实际上先理解为什么给、有什么风险，才是更高层次的药理思维。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating pharmacology as list memorization. In reality, the focus is mechanism and safety.
Common confusion 2: thinking side effects are secondary details. In reality, they often determine whether you should give the drug at all.
Common confusion 3: believing that knowing how to administer a drug is enough. Higher-level pharmacology means understanding why you are giving it and what risks come with it.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 8 — Vascular Access and Medication Administration
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 8 — Vascular Access and Medication Administration
reviews.summaryZh: 这章把药理从理论拉到执行，重点在 vascular access、fluids and electrolytes、IV/IO、dose/rate calculation 和 medication administration safety。它真正训练的是：怎么把“该给的东西”安全、准确地送进病人体内。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter pulls pharmacology from theory into execution, focusing on vascular access, fluids and electrolytes, IV/IO access, dose/rate calculation, and medication-administration safety. At its core, it teaches how to get the right thing into the patient safely and accurately.
highlightsZh: 理解 vascular access 的目的和风险
掌握 fluid / dose / rate calculation 的逻辑
把 asepsis、route、calculation 和 medication safety 绑在一起
highlightsEn: Understand the purpose and risks of vascular access
Learn the logic of fluid, dose, and rate calculation
Link asepsis, route, calculation, and medication safety together
keyPointsZh: Vascular access 的意义不只是“插上针”，而是建立可靠、安全的治疗通路。
IV/IO 和不同给药途径背后，核心都是如何把药物或液体准确送达。
fluids and electrolytes 不是单独章节知识，而是和 perfusion、shock、circulation 直接相关。
dose / rate calculation 出错会直接导致 underdose 或 overdose，是高风险错误类型。
asepsis、equipment disposal 和 routine precautions 都是 patient safety 的一部分，不是形式动作。
keyPointsEn: The purpose of vascular access is not simply “getting a line in,” but creating a reliable and safe treatment pathway.
Behind IV/IO and other routes is the same core question: how to deliver medication or fluid accurately.
Fluids and electrolytes are not isolated theory; they connect directly to perfusion, shock, and circulation.
Errors in dose or rate calculation can directly cause underdosing or overdosing and are therefore high-risk errors.
Asepsis, equipment disposal, and routine precautions are all parts of patient safety, not just ritual actions.
mustKnowZh: 要把 calculation 当成 patient safety skill，不是数学附属题。
要理解 route、concentration、dose、rate 是同一套安全问题。
要记住建立通路后仍然要 reassess，不是“成功插上就结束”。
mustKnowEn: Treat calculation as a patient-safety skill, not as a side math exercise.
Understand that route, concentration, dose, and rate are parts of the same safety problem.
Remember that establishing access is not the endpoint—ongoing reassessment is still required.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把这章理解成纯操作章。其实它同时是 calculation、安全和 clinical reasoning 章节。
常见误区 2：觉得能建立 IV 就代表任务完成。实际上后续通畅性、位置和进入效果同样重要。
常见误区 3：把 fluid administration 看成机械滴液。实际上它必须和病人的整体状态、适应证与风险绑在一起看。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating this as a pure procedure chapter. In reality, it is also about calculation, safety, and clinical reasoning.
Common confusion 2: thinking that once an IV is established, the job is done. In reality, patency, placement, and effect still matter.
Common confusion 3: viewing fluid administration as a mechanical drip task. In reality, it must be tied to the patient’s overall condition, indications, and risks.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲不同年龄层的人类发展特点，重点不是背年龄定义，而是理解不同阶段在解剖、生理、心理和沟通上的差异。它真正回答的是：为什么不同年龄的病人，不能用同一套 assessment 逻辑硬套。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers human development across age groups. The goal is not to memorize age labels, but to understand how anatomy, physiology, psychology, and communication differ by stage. At its core, it explains why patients of different ages cannot be forced into the same assessment logic.
highlightsZh: 理解不同年龄层不是同一种病人的缩放版
把发展阶段和沟通、评估、风险判断连起来
为 pediatrics、geriatrics 和 family interaction 打底
highlightsEn: Understand that age groups are not scaled versions of the same patient
Link developmental stage to communication, assessment, and risk judgment
Build the foundation for pediatrics, geriatrics, and family interaction
keyPointsZh: 婴儿、幼儿、学龄儿童、青少年、成人和老年人，在正常表现、风险和互动方式上都不同。
发展阶段会影响病史采集方式、合作程度、对疼痛和恐惧的表达、以及 family involvement 的重要性。
年龄相关差异不只是心理问题，也包括解剖和生理层面的真实差异。
“正常”生命体征、行为和反应方式，会随着年龄变化。
理解 human development 可以减少误判，例如把正常年龄表现误认成异常，或把危险表现误认成“只是小孩这样”。
keyPointsEn: Infants, toddlers, school-age children, adolescents, adults, and older adults differ in normal presentation, risk, and interaction style.
Developmental stage affects how history is gathered, how much cooperation is possible, how pain and fear are expressed, and how important family involvement may be.
Age-related differences are not only psychological; they also involve real anatomic and physiologic differences.
“Normal” vital signs, behavior, and response patterns change with age.
Understanding human development reduces misinterpretation—for example, mistaking age-appropriate behavior for pathology or missing dangerous findings by thinking “that’s just how kids are.”
mustKnowZh: 要把年龄层差异看成 assessment 基础，不是附加知识。
要知道 developmental stage 会直接影响 communication strategy。
后面的 pediatrics 和 geriatrics，很多判断都建立在这一章。
mustKnowEn: Treat age-related differences as part of the core assessment foundation, not as extra trivia.
Know that developmental stage directly affects communication strategy.
Much of the reasoning in later pediatrics and geriatrics is built on this chapter.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把儿童当缩小版成人。实际上 anatomy、physiology 和 behavior 都不同。
常见误区 2：把老年人只当“年纪大一点的成人”。实际上他们的 baseline、reserve 和 presentation 常很不一样。
常见误区 3：觉得 human development 只是理论内容。其实它会直接影响你现场如何问、如何看、如何判断。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating children as small adults. In reality, anatomy, physiology, and behavior all differ.
Common confusion 2: treating older adults as just older versions of younger adults. In reality, baseline, reserve, and presentation are often quite different.
Common confusion 3: thinking human development is only theory. In reality, it directly changes how you ask, observe, and judge in the field.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章重点在 patient communication：如何建立 rapport、怎么问问题、怎么看非语言讯号、以及在特殊场景下如何减少沟通失败。它真正训练的是：你不只是会说话，而是会通过沟通拿到临床上真正有用的信息。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter focuses on patient communication: how to build rapport, ask questions effectively, interpret nonverbal cues, and reduce communication failure in special situations. At its core, it trains you not just to speak, but to use communication to obtain clinically useful information.
highlightsZh: 把 communication 当成 assessment 工具
理解开放式问题和积极倾听的价值
注意 nonverbal cues 与情绪状态
highlightsEn: Treat communication as an assessment tool
Understand the value of open-ended questions and active listening
Pay attention to nonverbal cues and emotional state
keyPointsZh: 沟通不只是礼貌，而是 clinical assessment 的一部分。
开放式问题往往比封闭式问题更容易获得完整病史。
病人怎么说、什么时候停顿、有没有回避、是否紧张，本身都可能是临床线索。
非语言讯号（表情、姿势、语气、眼神、互动方式）常和病情、情绪、信任度相关。
特殊病人、敏感话题、高压力现场，都要求你调整沟通方式，而不是只重复同一句话。
keyPointsEn: Communication is not just politeness; it is part of clinical assessment.
Open-ended questions often obtain a fuller history than purely closed questions.
How the patient speaks, hesitates, avoids, or appears anxious may itself be clinically relevant.
Nonverbal cues—facial expression, posture, tone, eye contact, interaction style—often relate to illness, emotion, and trust.
Special patients, sensitive topics, and high-stress scenes require adjustment in communication style rather than simply repeating the same words.
mustKnowZh: 要把 rapport 建立看成 assessment 的第一步之一。
要记住 active listening 不是沉默，而是有方向地接信息。
要知道沟通方式会影响病人愿不愿意说、能不能说、以及你能不能得到真信息。
mustKnowEn: Building rapport should be seen as one of the first steps in assessment.
Remember that active listening is not passive silence; it is directed information gathering.
Know that communication style affects whether the patient is willing or able to share accurate information.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 communication 是软技能，和 clinical quality 分开。实际上问法会直接影响 diagnosis direction。
常见误区 2：以为说得越快越专业。实际上病人理解不了，就拿不到有效信息。
常见误区 3：只关注病人说出来的话，不看 nonverbal cues。这样会漏掉很多重要线索。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating communication as a soft skill separate from clinical quality. In reality, the way you ask questions shapes diagnostic direction.
Common confusion 2: thinking faster speech sounds more professional. In reality, if the patient cannot understand, useful information is lost.
Common confusion 3: focusing only on spoken words and ignoring nonverbal cues. That misses many important clues.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 research and quality，目标不是把你训练成学者，而是让你形成 evidence-based EMS 思维，知道系统如何衡量质量、回顾错误、改进流程。它回答的是：为什么“经验”还不够，你还要会看证据和系统表现。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter addresses research and quality. Its goal is not to turn you into an academic researcher, but to build an evidence-based EMS mindset and show how systems measure quality, review error, and improve processes. It answers why “experience” alone is not enough—you also need evidence and system awareness.
highlightsZh: 建立 evidence-based EMS 思维
理解 quality improvement 和 patient safety 的关系
知道研究设计与证据质量会影响实践
highlightsEn: Build an evidence-based EMS mindset
Understand the relationship between quality improvement and patient safety
Know that study design and evidence quality influence practice
keyPointsZh: 研究与质量管理的目标不是抽象学术，而是改善 patient outcomes 和 system performance。
evidence-based practice 强调把 best evidence、clinical judgment 和 patient context 放在一起。
quality improvement 不只是找人犯错，而是识别系统问题、流程问题和可改进点。
incident reporting、benchmarking、quality indicators 等概念，都是 system learning 的工具。
patient safety culture 的重点在于透明、学习和持续改进，而不是单纯 blame。
keyPointsEn: The purpose of research and quality work is not abstract scholarship, but improvement of patient outcomes and system performance.
Evidence-based practice combines the best evidence with clinical judgment and patient context.
Quality improvement is not just about finding blame; it is about identifying system problems, process problems, and areas for improvement.
Incident reporting, benchmarking, and quality indicators are tools for system learning.
A patient-safety culture emphasizes transparency, learning, and continuous improvement rather than simple blame.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 evidence-based practice 不是“只听论文”，而是 evidence + judgment + context。
要把 QI 看成 frontline provider 也参与的工作。
要明白 patient safety 是系统结果，不只是个人表现。
mustKnowEn: Know that evidence-based practice is not “just follow the literature,” but evidence + judgment + context.
Treat quality improvement as something frontline providers participate in, not only managers.
Understand that patient safety is a systems outcome, not just an individual trait.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把研究章节当成与现场无关。实际上它决定你为什么做现在做的事。
常见误区 2：把 QI 误解成追责工具。其实它更应该是学习工具。
常见误区 3：觉得经验比证据更高级。实际上经验如果不被验证，可能只是重复偏差。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating the research chapter as unrelated to field work. In reality, it explains why current practice exists.
Common confusion 2: misunderstanding quality improvement as a punishment tool. It should be a learning tool.
Common confusion 3: assuming experience is automatically superior to evidence. Experience without validation may simply repeat bias.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 12 — Expanded EMS Systems and Community Paramedicine in Canada
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 12 — Expanded EMS Systems and Community Paramedicine in Canada
reviews.summaryZh: 这章把 EMS 的视角从传统 response-and-transport 拉向 expanded systems 和 community paramedicine，讨论角色扩展、nontransport 和未来系统方向。它真正要你看到的是：EMS 不只是救护车，它也是健康系统的一部分。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter shifts the EMS perspective from traditional response-and-transport toward expanded systems and community paramedicine, covering role expansion, nontransport, and future system direction. Its deeper lesson is that EMS is not just an ambulance service—it is part of the wider health system.
highlightsZh: 认识 expanded EMS role
理解 community paramedicine 的价值
知道 nontransport 涉及 clinical、system 和 risk 三层判断
highlightsEn: Recognize the expanded EMS role
Understand the value of community paramedicine
Know that nontransport involves clinical, system, and risk-based judgment
keyPointsZh: 传统 EMS 模式强调 response 和 transport，但系统正在向更广泛的健康角色扩展。
community paramedicine 强调 prevention、follow-up、resource connection 和 chronic-care support。
expanded EMS systems 要求 provider 不只是会处理急性问题，也要理解系统 flow 和资源整合。
nontransport 不是简单地“不送”，而是一个和适应证、风险、follow-up 能力密切相关的决策。
这章会改变你对 EMS identity 的理解：从 emergency responder 扩展到 broader health-system participant。
keyPointsEn: Traditional EMS models emphasize response and transport, but the system is evolving toward broader health roles.
Community paramedicine emphasizes prevention, follow-up, resource connection, and chronic-care support.
Expanded EMS systems require providers to understand not just acute treatment, but also system flow and resource integration.
Nontransport is not simply “not taking the patient”; it is a decision closely tied to indication, risk, and follow-up capacity.
This chapter changes EMS identity from emergency responder alone to broader health-system participant.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 role expansion 不是“越做越多”而已，而是系统定位的改变。
要理解 nontransport 不是图省事，而是高要求 decision-making。
要把 community paramedicine 看成 EMS 和公共健康、primary care 的连接点。
mustKnowEn: Know that role expansion is not just “doing more,” but a shift in system position.
Understand that nontransport is not about convenience; it is high-level decision-making.
Treat community paramedicine as a bridge between EMS, public health, and primary care.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 EMS 只要会急救就够了。Chapter 12 在打破这种狭义理解。
常见误区 2：把 nontransport 理解成不负责。实际上它可能比 transport 更需要严密判断。
常见误区 3：觉得 community paramedicine 只是附加项目。其实它反映的是系统方向变化。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking EMS only needs acute emergency skills. Chapter 12 challenges that narrow view.
Common confusion 2: treating nontransport as careless or lazy. In reality, it may require more disciplined judgment than transport.
Common confusion 3: seeing community paramedicine as a side project. In reality, it reflects a larger systems-level shift.

reviews.summaryZh: 气道管理与通气是急救里最优先的生命支持问题，因为如果病人不能维持通畅气道和有效气体交换，就不可能长期存活。这一章教你的不是单纯背设备，而是学会尽早识别气道受损，分清气道问题、氧合问题和通气问题，并判断什么时候基础支持够用、什么时候必须立刻升级处理。核心目标是在病人彻底呼吸衰竭前就及时介入。
reviews.summaryEn: Airway management and ventilation are the first life-support priorities in emergency care because a patient cannot survive for long without a patent airway and effective gas exchange. This chapter teaches you how to recognize airway compromise early, distinguish airway problems from oxygenation and ventilation problems, and decide when basic support is enough versus when immediate escalation is required. The goal is not simply to memorize devices, but to understand how breathing fails, how patients compensate, and how to intervene before complete respiratory collapse occurs.
highlightsZh: 病人即使还在呼吸、还能说话，也可能正在走向呼吸衰竭。
气道、氧合、通气彼此相关，但不是同一个概念。
呼吸工作量、疲劳、意识状态和气道保护能力，往往比单个监测数字更早暴露危险。
吸引、体位、气道辅助器、给氧和辅助通气分别是在解决不同层级的问题。
真正的能力不是会摆设备，而是能及早看出失败并按正确顺序升级支持。
highlightsEn: A patient may still be breathing and talking while moving toward respiratory failure.
Airway, oxygenation, and ventilation are related but not identical concepts.
Work of breathing, fatigue, mental status, and airway protection often reveal danger earlier than a single monitor number.
Suction, positioning, airway adjuncts, oxygen, and assisted ventilation each solve different problems.
The real skill is recognizing failure early and escalating support in the right order.
keyPointsZh: Airway 指的是空气能不能实际进出呼吸道；oxygenation 指氧气能不能进入血液；ventilation 指病人能不能把空气有效地进出肺部、完成氧气与二氧化碳交换。这三者相关，但一个出问题不代表另外两个一定同样严重。
气道受损可能来自软组织阻塞、舌后坠、血液、呕吐物、分泌物、水肿、创伤或异物。意识下降之所以危险，是因为病人可能在完全堵塞前，就已经先失去保护气道的能力。
气道受损的常见线索包括鼾声、痰鸣音、喉鸣、可见阻塞、无法处理分泌物、血液或呕吐物，以及明显的气道保护能力下降。这些线索不只是告诉你“有问题”，还提示你更可能需要哪一类处理。
病人即使气道通，也可能氧合失败，因为肺部本身无法有效交换气体。反过来，病人也可能暂时还有氧合，但其实通气已经不行，尤其当潮气量不足、疲劳上升、二氧化碳排不出去时。
呼吸工作量是呼吸评估里价值最高的部分之一。要看辅助呼吸肌使用、体位改变、浅快呼吸、说话断句、鼻翼扇动、三凹征、明显缺气感和疲劳表现。很多时候，这些危险线索会早于 pulse oximetry 的明显下降出现。
疲劳是一个重要转折点。病人如果从一开始非常用力呼吸，后来突然显得“安静一点”，不一定是在好转，也可能是在逐渐失去维持通气的能力。所以重复 reassessment 非常关键。
Suction 绝不是小技能。在很多 airway emergency 里，吸引正是后续所有气道处理得以进行的前提，因为它能清掉阻碍视野和气流的血液、呕吐物和分泌物。
Airway adjuncts 是支撑工具，不是万能解法。它们的作用是在病人自己不能稳定维持气道时帮忙撑住，但并不能取代临床判断，也不能解决所有呼吸问题。
Oxygen therapy 的目标是改善氧输送，但单纯给氧并不能修复通气不足。如果病人本身空气进出得很差，即使已经在给氧，也可能仍然需要辅助通气。
辅助通气应该在病人已经不能维持有效通气时就开始考虑，而不是一定要等到完全停呼吸才接手。拖延是气道处理中最经典的错误之一。
高级气道管理是升级步骤，不是所有气道处理的起点。好的 airway management 总是先从评估、体位、吸引、基础手法、给氧支持，以及判断病人是在代偿还是在走向失败开始。
所有气道干预最终都服务于同一个目标：保持气道通畅、维持氧合、支持通气，并阻止病人进入完全呼吸衰竭。
keyPointsEn: Airway refers to whether air can physically move into and out of the respiratory tract. Oxygenation refers to whether oxygen reaches the blood. Ventilation refers to whether the patient is moving air effectively enough to exchange oxygen and carbon dioxide. A patient can have one of these problems without having all three at the same degree.

Airway compromise may come from soft-tissue obstruction, the tongue falling back, blood, vomit, secretions, swelling, trauma, or foreign bodies. A reduced level of consciousness is dangerous because the patient may lose the ability to protect the airway even before complete obstruction occurs.

Signs of airway compromise include snoring, gurgling, stridor, visible obstruction, poor airway protection, pooling secretions, and an inability to handle blood or vomit. These clues matter because they tell you not only that something is wrong, but also what type of intervention is most likely to help.

A patient may have a patent airway but still fail oxygenation because the lungs are not exchanging gas effectively. Likewise, a patient may be oxygenating temporarily but ventilating poorly, especially if tidal volume is low, fatigue is rising, or carbon dioxide clearance is failing.

Work of breathing is one of the highest-value parts of respiratory assessment. Look for accessory muscle use, posture changes, shallow breathing, inability to speak in full sentences, nasal flaring, retractions, air hunger, and visible exhaustion. These signs often warn you before pulse oximetry shows obvious collapse.

Fatigue is a major turning point. A patient who looks slightly calmer after extreme respiratory effort may not be improving; the patient may be tiring out and losing the ability to maintain ventilation. This is why repeated reassessment is essential.
Suction is not a minor skill. In many airway emergencies, suction is what makes the rest of airway care possible by removing blood, vomit, and secretions that block visualization and airflow.

Airway adjuncts are support tools, not magical fixes. Their job is to help maintain patency when the patient cannot do it reliably. They do not replace judgment, and they do not solve every respiratory problem.

Oxygen therapy is used to improve oxygen delivery, but oxygen alone does not fix inadequate ventilation. If the patient is moving air poorly, assisted ventilation may be required even if oxygen is already being delivered.
Assisted ventilation should be considered when the patient is no longer maintaining effective ventilation, not only when breathing has fully stopped. Waiting too long is a classic airway error.

Advanced airway management is an escalation step, not the starting point of all airway care. Good airway management always begins with assessment, positioning, suction, basic maneuvers, oxygen support, and recognition of whether the patient is still compensating or beginning to fail.
The purpose of every airway intervention is the same: preserve airway patency, maintain oxygenation, support ventilation, and prevent the patient from progressing to complete respiratory failure.
mustKnowZh: 你必须清楚分开气道问题、氧合问题和通气问题。混在一起想，处理就很容易错。
不要因为病人还能说话就低估风险。会说话只是线索，不是稳定保证。
呼吸工作量、疲劳和意识状态，很多时候和监测数字一样重要，甚至更早提示危险。
辅助通气不只是给完全停呼吸的病人，它也适用于已经不能有效通气的病人。
气道管理是一条逐级升级的支持链，而不是设备清单。
mustKnowEn: You must clearly separate airway problems from oxygenation and ventilation problems. If you confuse them, you will choose the wrong intervention.
Do not let a speaking patient fool you into underestimating risk. Speech is a clue, not a guarantee of stability.
Work of breathing, fatigue, and mental status often matter as much as monitor readings.
Assisted ventilation is not only for apnea. It is for failure of effective ventilation.
Airway management is an escalating sequence of support, not a device checklist.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：病人还能说话，就说明气道没事。实际上病人可能已经在疲劳、在肿胀、在出血，或者已经快失去气道保护能力。
常见误区 2：给氧就等于处理了呼吸窘迫。给氧也许能改善氧合，但并不会自动修复通气不足。
常见误区 3：pulse oximeter 看起来还行，病人就安全。实际上病人仍可能正在疲劳、通气变差，或即将快速恶化。
常见误区 4：气道管理就是高级气道。事实上，大多数 airway management 都是从识别、体位、吸引和基础支持开始的。
常见误区 5：辅助通气要等到完全停呼吸才上。实际上等到那时，往往已经错过了更安全的介入窗口。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: If the patient is talking, the airway is fine. In reality, a patient may still be tiring, swelling, bleeding, or losing airway protection.
Common confusion 2: Oxygen fixes respiratory distress. Oxygen may improve oxygenation, but it does not automatically fix poor ventilation.
Common confusion 3: If the pulse oximeter looks okay, the patient is safe. A patient may still be tiring out, ventilating poorly, or heading toward rapid deterioration.
Common confusion 4: Airway management means advanced airway placement. In reality, most airway management begins with recognition, positioning, suction, and basic support.
Common confusion 5: Assisted ventilation should wait until full respiratory arrest. In reality, waiting that long may mean you have already missed the safest intervention window.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章是 Chapter 13 的进阶版，重点是机械通气、辅助通气、ventilator management 和持续监测。它真正处理的是：当病人已经不能靠基础手段维持呼吸时，怎么更稳定地支持他。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter is the advanced extension of Chapter 13, focusing on mechanical ventilation, assisted ventilation, ventilator management, and ongoing monitoring. At its core, it addresses how to support a patient more reliably when basic measures are no longer enough.
highlightsZh: 从基础通气走向高级呼吸支持
理解 ventilator management 不只是设参数
重视 patient response 与持续监测
highlightsEn: Move from basic ventilation to advanced respiratory support
Understand that ventilator management is more than setting numbers
Emphasize patient response and ongoing monitoring
keyPointsZh: 机械通气代表病人更重、管理更复杂、错误代价更大。
通气支持不是“开起来就完”，而是要持续看氧合、通气、病人表现和设备反应。
ventilator management 必须把机器参数和病人生理反应一起看。
高级呼吸支持的意义不是炫技，而是更稳定地维持生命所需的气体交换。
keyPointsEn: Mechanical ventilation usually means the patient is more critically ill, management is more complex, and errors are more costly.
Ventilatory support is not “turn it on and walk away”; oxygenation, ventilation, patient presentation, and machine response all require monitoring.
Ventilator management requires reading machine settings together with the patient’s physiology.
The point of advanced respiratory support is not technical performance for its own sake, but more stable maintenance of gas exchange.
mustKnowZh: 要把 Chapter 14 看成“高级 respiratory support chapter”。
要知道 patient monitoring 在这一章里比设备本身还重要。
要明白高阶 support 的核心仍然是病人，而不是机器。
mustKnowEn: Treat Chapter 14 as the advanced respiratory-support chapter.
Know that patient monitoring matters even more than the machine itself here.
Understand that the core of high-level support is still the patient, not the device.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 mechanical ventilation 就只是更复杂的 bagging。实际上它需要持续管理。
常见误区 2：以为参数正确就说明病人稳定。实际上病人表现永远更重要。
常见误区 3：把 ventilator management 当成纯设备章节。其实它依然是 clinical judgment chapter。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking mechanical ventilation is just more complicated bagging. In reality, it requires ongoing management.
Common confusion 2: assuming that correct settings mean the patient is stable. In reality, patient presentation still matters most.
Common confusion 3: treating ventilator management as a machine chapter only. It remains a chapter about clinical judgment.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章在讲 patient history：怎么从病人、家属、旁观者和场景中拿到真正有诊断价值的信息。它的核心不是问得多，而是问得对、问得准。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter is about patient history: how to obtain diagnostically useful information from the patient, family, bystanders, and the scene. Its core is not asking more questions, but asking the right questions in the right way.
highlightsZh: history 会决定后续 physical exam 方向
开放式提问和 active listening 很关键
敏感问题也要会问，但要用对方式
highlightsEn: History guides the later physical exam
Open-ended questions and active listening are crucial
Sensitive issues still need to be asked—but in the right way
keyPointsZh: history 不是形式流程，而是后续判断方向的入口。
你问什么、怎么问、什么时候问，会直接影响你得到的信息质量。
病人、家属、旁观者和场景本身都可能是信息来源。
sensitive topics 不能跳过，但方式必须尊重、清楚并尽量保护隐私。
好的病史会提高 focused exam 的效率和准确度。
keyPointsEn: History is not just a formal step; it is the entry point for later clinical reasoning.
What you ask, how you ask, and when you ask directly affect the quality of information you receive.
The patient, family, bystanders, and the scene may all be information sources.
Sensitive topics should not be skipped, but they must be approached respectfully, clearly, and as privately as possible.
A good history improves the efficiency and accuracy of the focused exam.
mustKnowZh: 要把 history 当成 assessment 的第一扇门。
要知道主诉只是入口，不是全部。
要记住 history 的质量会直接影响 diagnosis direction。
mustKnowEn: Treat history as the first door into assessment.
Know that the chief complaint is only the entry point, not the whole story.
Remember that the quality of the history directly shapes the diagnostic direction.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 history 理解成固定问答模板。实际上它需要根据病人和场景调整。
常见误区 2：以为查体比 history 更“硬核”。实际上没有好 history，查体方向也会偏。
常见误区 3：觉得敏感问题最好别问。实际上关键问题还是得问，只是方式要专业。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating history as a fixed script. In reality, it must be adapted to the patient and scene.
Common confusion 2: thinking the physical exam is more “real” than the history. In reality, without a good history, the exam may drift in the wrong direction.
Common confusion 3: assuming it is best to avoid sensitive questions. In reality, important questions still need to be asked—just professionally.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 physical examination：如何系统地看、摸、听、量，并把 findings 组织成 clinical picture。重点不是“做动作”，而是把动作变成判断。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter focuses on the physical examination: how to systematically inspect, palpate, auscultate, measure, and organize findings into a clinical picture. The point is not simply to perform maneuvers, but to turn maneuvers into judgment.
highlightsZh: general impression 很关键
vital signs 要看趋势而不是单次数值
physical exam 要和 history 结合
highlightsEn: General impression is highly important
Vital signs should be read as trends, not isolated numbers
The physical exam must be integrated with the history
keyPointsZh: general impression 常在第一眼就帮助判断病人 sick or not sick。
physical exam 不只是摸一遍，而是系统收集可解释的体征。
vital signs 的价值在于趋势、组合和 context，不是单个数字。
儿科和特殊病人的 physical exam 不能机械套成人方式。
physical exam 有局限，所以一定要和 history、scene clues、ongoing assessment 一起看。
keyPointsEn: General impression often helps decide very early whether the patient is sick or not sick.
The physical exam is not just “touching around,” but a systematic collection of interpretable findings.
Vital signs matter as trends, combinations, and context—not as isolated numbers.
Pediatric and special-population exams cannot be copied mechanically from adult methods.
The physical exam has limits, so it must be integrated with history, scene clues, and ongoing assessment.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 first impression 会改变后续 assessment 节奏。
要把 vital signs 视为动态线索。
要记住 exam 本身不能脱离 context。
mustKnowEn: Know that first impression can change the pace of the whole assessment.
Treat vital signs as dynamic clues.
Remember that the exam itself cannot be separated from context.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把查体当成 checklist。实际上它应该服务于临床判断。
常见误区 2：只做“会痛的地方”。实际上最危险的问题可能不在那里。
常见误区 3：只看一次 vital signs 就下结论。趋势往往更重要。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating the exam as a checklist. In reality, it should serve clinical judgment.
Common confusion 2: examining only “where it hurts.” The most dangerous issue may be elsewhere.
Common confusion 3: drawing conclusions from a single set of vital signs. Trends are often more important.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章是 assessment 方法论主轴，把 scene assessment、initial assessment、focused exam、detailed exam 和 ongoing assessment 串成完整流程。它真正训练的是：怎么从零散信息走到有顺序的临床判断。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter is the methodological core of assessment, linking scene assessment, initial assessment, focused exam, detailed exam, and ongoing assessment into one complete process. At its core, it trains you to move from scattered information to structured clinical judgment.
highlightsZh: assessment 是动态流程
scene clues 会改变优先级
ongoing assessment 很关键
highlightsEn: Assessment is a dynamic process
Scene clues change priorities
Ongoing assessment is critical
keyPointsZh: scene assessment 会在接触病人前就提供高价值风险和机制信息。
initial assessment 决定优先级与病人严重度初步判断。
focused exam 和 detailed exam 不同，前者更强调方向性和效率。
ongoing assessment 的价值在于病人会变化，而不是静止不动。
assessment 有框架，但执行顺序会根据 stability 和 scene 变化。
keyPointsEn: Scene assessment provides high-value risk and mechanism information even before direct patient contact.
The initial assessment sets priorities and provides an early sense of acuity.
A focused exam differs from a detailed exam; the focused exam emphasizes direction and efficiency.
Ongoing assessment matters because patients change—they are not static.
Assessment has structure, but the sequence may shift depending on stability and scene conditions.
mustKnowZh: 要把 Chapter 17 看成整本书的方法论主轴。
要知道 assessment 是“有结构的动态流程”。
要记住 reassessment 不是重复劳动，而是临床安全机制。
mustKnowEn: Treat Chapter 17 as one of the core methodology chapters of the whole book.
Know that assessment is a “structured but dynamic process.”
Remember that reassessment is not redundant work—it is a clinical safety mechanism.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 assessment 当成固定顺序背诵。实际上顺序会被病情严重度打断和重排。
常见误区 2：以为做完一次就结束。实际上很多病人的真正问题是“变化趋势”。
常见误区 3：觉得 scene assessment 只是安全流程。其实它也会改变诊断和 transport 思路。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating assessment as a fixed memorized sequence. In reality, acuity can interrupt and reorder it.
Common confusion 2: assuming one assessment is enough. In reality, many patients are defined by change over time.
Common confusion 3: thinking scene assessment is only a safety step. It can also change diagnostic and transport thinking.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章是把知识变成判断的章节，重点在 critical thinking、priority setting 和从 theory 到 street application。它真正处理的是：同样会背知识，为什么有的人能做出对的决定，有的人不行。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter is about turning knowledge into judgment, focusing on critical thinking, priority setting, and moving from theory to street application. At its core, it asks why two people with the same facts may make very different clinical decisions.
highlightsZh: 从“会做”升级到“会判断”
最可能诊断不一定等于最重要行动
protocol 要和 patient context 一起看
highlightsEn: Move from “performing” to “judging”
The most likely diagnosis is not always the most important next action
Protocols must be read together with patient context
keyPointsZh: clinical decision-making 的核心是 priority，而不只是 diagnosis label。
真正的决定常常由 immediate threat、transport urgency 和 available resources 驱动。
protocol 提供框架，但 patient context 决定怎么应用。
critical thinking 要求你把 signs、history、scene 和 risk 一起整合。
clinical reasoning 是从理论走向 street-level practice 的桥。
keyPointsEn: The core of clinical decision-making is priority, not just assigning a diagnosis label.
Real decisions are often driven by immediate threat, transport urgency, and available resources.
Protocols provide structure, but patient context determines application.
Critical thinking requires integrating signs, history, scene, and risk together.
Clinical reasoning is the bridge from theory to street-level practice.
mustKnowZh: 要知道“最重要下一步”常常比“最准确诊断名称”更优先。
要把 protocol 和 judgment 同时保留，不偏向任何一边。
要把 critical thinking 看成可训练能力，而不是天赋。
mustKnowEn: Know that the “most important next step” is often a higher priority than the “most precise diagnostic name.”
Keep both protocol and judgment in view rather than leaning entirely on one side.
Treat critical thinking as a trainable skill, not as a mysterious talent.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 diagnosis 最重要。实际上 priority 常常更重要。
常见误区 2：觉得有 protocol 就不用想。实际上 protocol 只是框架。
常见误区 3：把 critical thinking 理解成复杂推理。很多时候它只是更清楚地判断什么最危险。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: assuming diagnosis is always the main goal. In reality, priority often matters more.
Common confusion 2: assuming a protocol means no thinking is required. In reality, protocol is only a framework.
Common confusion 3: treating critical thinking as complicated theory. Often it is simply the disciplined recognition of what is most dangerous.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 communications and documentation，核心在于把你看到的、做过的、判断过的内容，准确传达给后续团队并留下清楚记录。它真正要你理解的是：clinical care 不是你做完就结束，而是要被别人接得住。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers communications and documentation, focusing on accurately passing along what you saw, did, and decided while leaving a clear record. At its core, it teaches that clinical care does not end when you finish—it must also be receivable by the next team.
highlightsZh: handoff 不只是说内容，还包括排序
documentation 是 continuity of care 的一部分
radio communication 会影响资源和接收准备
highlightsEn: Handoff is not only about content but also about ordering
Documentation is part of continuity of care
Radio communication affects resources and receiving-facility preparation
keyPointsZh: communication 的目标不是“我已经讲过了”，而是“对方真的听懂并能接手”。
radio communication 会改变 receiving team 和 system resource preparation。
documentation 不只是 legal protection，也是 clinical continuity 工具。
记录内容必须反映你实际看到什么、做了什么、病人怎么变。
handoff 质量会直接影响后续 treatment quality。
keyPointsEn: The goal of communication is not “I said it,” but “the other team truly understood and can act on it.”
Radio communication changes how the receiving team and the system prepare their resources.
Documentation is not only legal protection; it is also a continuity-of-care tool.
The record must reflect what you actually saw, what you did, and how the patient changed.
The quality of the handoff directly affects the quality of the next phase of treatment.
mustKnowZh: 要把 documentation 当成 patient care 本身的一部分。
要知道 handoff 的顺序和简洁度同样重要。
要记住 communication failure 也会造成 patient harm。
mustKnowEn: Treat documentation as part of patient care itself.
Know that order and clarity matter in handoff.
Remember that communication failure can also cause patient harm.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得 documentation 只是为了法律。实际上它对后续照护也一样关键。
常见误区 2：觉得 handoff 只要把所有信息堆出来就行。实际上重点是优先级排序。
常见误区 3：觉得 communication skill 和 clinical skill 是两回事。Chapter 19 说明它们其实绑在一起。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: thinking documentation exists only for legal reasons. In reality, it is just as important for ongoing care.
Common confusion 2: assuming handoff means dumping all information at once. In reality, prioritization matters.
Common confusion 3: thinking communication skill and clinical skill are separate. Chapter 19 shows that they are tightly linked.

reviews.titleZh: Chapter 20 — Trauma Systems and Mechanism of Injury
reviews.titleEn: Chapter 20 — Trauma Systems and Mechanism of Injury
reviews.summaryZh: 这章是 trauma 主线的入口，重点在 trauma systems、mechanism of injury、能量传递和多发伤思维。它真正训练的是：在还没完全看到伤之前，就先推理病人可能伤到哪里。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter is the entry point to the trauma pathway, focusing on trauma systems, mechanism of injury, energy transfer, and multisystem injury thinking. At its core, it trains you to infer where the patient may be injured even before all injuries are obvious.
highlightsZh: MOI 是推理工具，不只是背景资料
高能量机制要警惕隐藏伤
trauma systems 讲的是资源匹配和 destination thinking
highlightsEn: MOI is a reasoning tool, not just background information
High-energy mechanisms raise concern for hidden injury
Trauma systems are about resource matching and destination thinking
keyPointsZh: 受伤机制（MOI）可以帮助你在体征还不完整时先建立损伤模型。
能量越高，潜在内部损伤或多系统损伤的风险通常越高。
创伤系统的意义不只是运送病人，而是把病人送到最合适层级的 definitive care。
外表“看起来还好”不代表内部损伤轻。
Trauma assessment 一开始就要把 scene、mechanism、resources 和 transport decision 一起看。
keyPointsEn: Mechanism of injury helps you build an early injury model even before all signs are obvious.
The greater the energy transfer, the greater the concern for internal injury or multisystem trauma.
The purpose of trauma systems is not merely transport, but transport to the most appropriate level of definitive care.
A patient who “looks okay” externally may still have serious internal injury.
From the start, trauma assessment must integrate scene, mechanism, resources, and transport decisions.
mustKnowZh: 要把 MOI 看成 high-value clue，不是附属描述。
要知道 trauma destination choice 会影响 outcome。
要明白 scene 和 mechanism 会改变你对病人严重度的初始判断。
mustKnowEn: Treat MOI as a high-value clue, not an optional description.
Know that trauma destination choice affects outcome.
Understand that scene and mechanism can strongly change your initial judgment of severity.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得体表没大伤就说明问题不大。实际上机制可能已经提示严重内伤。
常见误区 2：把 MOI 当成考试背景信息。实际上它是 trauma reasoning 的核心。
常见误区 3：认为最近医院一定最好。实际上 trauma systems 强调“最合适层级”而不是“最近”。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: assuming minimal external injury means minimal overall injury. The mechanism may still suggest severe internal damage.
Common confusion 2: treating MOI as background trivia. In reality, it is central to trauma reasoning.
Common confusion 3: assuming the nearest hospital is always best. Trauma systems emphasize the most appropriate level of care, not just proximity.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 bleeding and shock，是 trauma 里最致命的主线之一。重点在出血、灌注失败、休克识别和早期干预。它真正要你抓住的是：失血不是只少一点血，而是整个氧输送系统开始崩。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers bleeding and shock, one of the most lethal pathways in trauma. It focuses on hemorrhage, perfusion failure, shock recognition, and early intervention. At its core, it teaches that blood loss is not just “losing some blood,” but the beginning of failure in the entire oxygen-delivery system.
highlightsZh: 不要等到晚期低血压才想到 shock
compensated shock 容易被低估
骨盆与隐匿性出血要特别警惕
highlightsEn: Do not wait for late hypotension to think about shock
Compensated shock is easy to underestimate
Be especially alert to pelvic and hidden hemorrhage
keyPointsZh: 出血性休克的危险不只是 volume loss，而是 perfusion failure 和组织缺氧。
病人可能在看起来还行的时候已经进入 compensated shock。
低血压常是较晚期表现，不能拿它当唯一识别标准。
隐匿性出血（尤其胸腔、腹腔、骨盆）是高风险陷阱。
bleeding control、rapid recognition 和 destination thinking 会直接影响结局。
keyPointsEn: The danger of hemorrhagic shock is not just volume loss, but perfusion failure and tissue hypoxia.
A patient may already be in compensated shock while still appearing relatively okay.
Hypotension is often a late finding and cannot be your only recognition tool.
Hidden hemorrhage—especially in the chest, abdomen, and pelvis—is a major trap.
Bleeding control, rapid recognition, and destination thinking directly affect outcome.
mustKnowZh: 要把 shock 理解成 perfusion problem，而不是只看血压。
要记住 compensated shock 会骗人。
要知道 hidden bleeding 是 trauma 里最危险的坑之一。
mustKnowEn: Understand shock as a perfusion problem, not just a blood-pressure issue.
Remember that compensated shock is deceptive.
Know that hidden bleeding is one of the most dangerous trauma traps.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：以为血压还行就不算 shock。实际上很多病人已经在代偿。
常见误区 2：把出血只理解成外出血。实际上内出血更容易误判。
常见误区 3：以为休克识别只靠 monitor。实际上病人外观、皮肤、精神状态也很重要。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: assuming that if the blood pressure is okay, there is no shock. Many patients are still compensating.
Common confusion 2: treating bleeding as external bleeding only. Internal hemorrhage is often easier to miss.
Common confusion 3: assuming shock recognition comes only from the monitor. Appearance, skin, and mental status matter too.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 soft-tissue injuries，包括开放伤、闭合伤、挤压伤和爆炸伤。重点在于：软组织伤看起来常见，但严重度常被低估。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers soft-tissue injuries, including open wounds, closed wounds, crush injury, and blast injury. Its key lesson is that soft-tissue injury may look common, but its severity is often underestimated.
highlightsZh: 软组织伤不只是皮肤问题
爆炸伤是多机制伤
重点是控制出血、保护组织和 reassessment
highlightsEn: Soft-tissue injury is more than a skin problem
Blast injury is a multi-mechanism injury
Management centers on hemorrhage control, tissue protection, and reassessment
keyPointsZh: 软组织损伤可能伴随大量失血、感染风险和深层组织损伤。
开放伤和闭合伤都可能严重，不能只看是不是“有洞”。
挤压伤和爆炸伤常常有超出表面所见的复杂损伤。
伤口处理的目标不只是盖住，而是控制出血、保护组织、减少进一步损伤。
评估时要同时看 wound appearance、bleeding、pain、function 和 hidden injury potential。
keyPointsEn: Soft-tissue injuries may involve major blood loss, infection risk, and deep tissue damage.
Both open and closed injuries can be severe; do not judge only by whether the skin is open.
Crush and blast injuries often involve complex damage beyond what is immediately visible.
The goal of wound care is not simply covering the wound, but controlling hemorrhage, protecting tissue, and preventing further harm.
Assessment should consider wound appearance, bleeding, pain, function, and the possibility of hidden injury.
mustKnowZh: 要知道 soft-tissue injury 可能非常 serious。
要记住 blast injury 不只是表皮问题。
要把 tissue protection 和 reassessment 放进处理目标。
mustKnowEn: Know that soft-tissue injuries can be very serious.
Remember that blast injury is not just a superficial problem.
Include tissue protection and reassessment in your management goals.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：觉得软组织伤只是“小伤”。实际上它可能出血很多或合并深层损伤。
常见误区 2：以为看起来小就代表问题小。实际严重度常常被表面掩盖。
常见误区 3：只关注 wound 外观，不看功能和整体状况。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating soft-tissue injuries as “minor wounds.” They may involve major bleeding or deeper injury.
Common confusion 2: assuming a small-looking wound means a small problem. Surface appearance may hide real severity.
Common confusion 3: focusing only on wound appearance and ignoring function and overall condition.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 burns，核心不是“皮肤烧坏了多少”，而是烧伤如何影响全身生理、液体平衡、气道和长期结局。它真正训练的是把烧伤当 systemic problem 来看。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers burns. Its core is not just “how much skin is burned,” but how burns affect whole-body physiology, fluid balance, the airway, and long-term outcomes. It trains you to view burns as systemic problems.
highlightsZh: 烧伤不是只看面积
深度、位置和吸入伤都重要
burns 可能很快变成 circulation / airway problem
highlightsEn: Burns are not judged by size alone
Depth, location, and inhalation injury all matter
Burns may rapidly become circulation or airway problems
keyPointsZh: 烧伤严重度来自面积、深度、部位和吸入伤风险的综合。
烧伤会影响 fluid balance、perfusion、pain control 和 infection risk。
某些特殊部位烧伤即使面积不大，也可能非常高风险。
吸入伤和气道受累会显著改变优先级。
burn center thinking 的重点是识别谁需要更高层级资源。
keyPointsEn: Burn severity depends on the combination of size, depth, location, and inhalation risk.
Burns affect fluid balance, perfusion, pain, and infection risk.
Certain special-location burns may be high risk even when total area is not large.
Inhalation injury and airway involvement strongly change priorities.
Burn-center thinking is about recognizing who needs a higher level of specialized care.
mustKnowZh: 不要把 burns 当成皮肤章节。
面积重要，但不是唯一判断标准。
要始终考虑 airway 与 fluid problem。
mustKnowEn: Do not treat burns as only a skin chapter.
Size matters, but it is not the only decision factor.
Always think about airway and fluid issues as well.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：只看 BSA，不看深度和部位。
常见误区 2：认为烧伤只要处理疼痛。实际上它可能迅速变成 systemic emergency。
常见误区 3：忽略 inhalation injury，因为皮肤外观看起来没那么重。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: looking only at BSA and ignoring depth and location.
Common confusion 2: assuming burn care is mainly pain control. In reality, burns may quickly become systemic emergencies.
Common confusion 3: missing inhalation injury because the skin injury does not look dramatic.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 head and face injuries，把颅脑、面部、眼耳口腔和前颈部损伤串起来。核心在于：这些区域的损伤不只影响外观，还可能迅速影响 airway、neurologic function 和整体稳定度。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers head and face injuries, linking brain, facial, eye, ear, oral, and anterior neck injury. Its core message is that these injuries affect far more than appearance—they may rapidly affect the airway, neurologic function, and overall stability.
highlightsZh: face injury 可能迅速变成 airway issue
head injury assessment 要盯 neurologic deterioration
不要被外观分散，深层风险更重要
highlightsEn: Face injury can quickly become an airway issue
Head-injury assessment must watch for neurologic deterioration
Do not be distracted by appearance; deeper risk matters more
keyPointsZh: 头面部损伤往往同时涉及 airway、bleeding、neuro status 和 sensory organs。
head injury assessment 的重点之一是识别 TBI 和 ongoing neurologic change。
面部损伤会通过出血、肿胀和解剖变形影响 airway。
眼耳口腔和前颈部都可能提供高价值 injury clues。
这种病人外观看起来很 dramatic，但真正优先级要回到 airway / neuro / circulation。
keyPointsEn: Head and face injuries often involve the airway, bleeding, neurologic status, and sensory organs at the same time.
A major goal of head-injury assessment is recognizing TBI and ongoing neurologic change.
Facial trauma can affect the airway through bleeding, swelling, and structural distortion.
The eyes, ears, mouth, and anterior neck may all provide high-value injury clues.
These patients may look dramatic externally, but priorities must still return to airway, neurologic status, and circulation.
mustKnowZh: 要把 head and face injury 当 high-priority trauma 看。
要知道 neurologic change 和 airway risk 会相互交织。
要记住别被“看起来吓人”或“看起来没事”这两种表象骗到。
mustKnowEn: Treat head and face injuries as high-priority trauma.
Know that neurologic change and airway risk often intertwine.
Remember not to be fooled by either “dramatic-looking” or “not that bad-looking” appearances.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 face injury 当 cosmetic problem。实际上它可能很快影响 airway。
常见误区 2：只看有没有出血，不看 neuro change。
常见误区 3：被外观吸走全部注意力，反而忽略真正危及生命的 priority。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating facial injury as mainly cosmetic. In reality, it may quickly affect the airway.
Common confusion 2: looking only for bleeding and ignoring neurologic change.
Common confusion 3: letting dramatic appearance consume attention while missing the real life threats.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 spine injuries，核心在于识别潜在脊柱与脊髓损伤风险，并避免在救治和搬运中造成进一步伤害。它真正训练的是：不要把脊柱管理理解成机械化固定，而要理解保护神经功能的逻辑。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers spine injuries, focusing on recognizing possible spinal and spinal-cord injury while avoiding additional harm during care and movement. At its core, it teaches that spinal management is not just mechanical immobilization, but the protection of neurologic function.
highlightsZh: spine injury assessment 不能只看疼痛位置
机制、神经体征和风险背景都很重要
核心目标是减少进一步神经损害
highlightsEn: Spine injury assessment cannot rely only on pain location
Mechanism, neurologic findings, and risk context all matter
The core goal is reducing further neurologic injury
keyPointsZh: spine injury 风险识别要结合 mechanism、symptoms、neurologic findings 和 overall context。
脊柱损伤管理的重点不是“固定动作本身”，而是减少 movement-related harm。
神经体征和功能变化往往比单纯疼痛更有分量。
移动和搬运方式本身可能改变病人结局。
spinal protection 是 trauma handling 的一部分，不是独立的孤岛章节。
keyPointsEn: Recognition of spine injury requires integrating mechanism, symptoms, neurologic findings, and overall context.
The focus of spinal management is not the immobilization maneuver itself, but reducing movement-related harm.
Neurologic findings and functional change often matter more than pain alone.
The way a patient is moved and handled may change the outcome.
Spinal protection is part of trauma handling overall, not an isolated separate skill.
mustKnowZh: 要记住脊柱管理的核心是 neurologic protection。
要知道 mechanism 和 neuro findings 常常比局部疼痛更重要。
要把 movement 也当成 intervention 风险来看。
mustKnowEn: Remember that the core of spinal management is neurologic protection.
Know that mechanism and neurologic findings often matter more than localized pain.
Treat movement itself as a potential intervention risk.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 spinal management 等于“看到板子就上板子”。实际上核心是风险判断。
常见误区 2：以为病人没喊背痛就排除脊柱伤。
常见误区 3：把搬运当成技术细节，其实它直接关系到 secondary injury。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: equating spinal management with automatic board use. In reality, the core is risk assessment.
Common confusion 2: assuming the absence of back pain rules out spinal injury.
Common confusion 3: treating movement as a technical detail when it directly relates to secondary injury.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 thoracic injuries，重点在胸部损伤如何快速影响 breathing、oxygenation 和 circulation。它真正训练的是：把 thoracic trauma 看成会迅速变成 life threat 的问题。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers thoracic injuries, focusing on how chest trauma can rapidly affect breathing, oxygenation, and circulation. Its deeper lesson is to treat thoracic trauma as something that can quickly become life-threatening.
highlightsZh: 胸部创伤与 airway / breathing 高度相关
呼吸困难、胸痛、呼吸音变化都很重要
thoracic injuries 常快速变成 life threat
highlightsEn: Thoracic trauma is closely linked to airway and breathing
Dyspnea, chest pain, and breath-sound changes are important clues
Thoracic injuries often become life threats quickly
keyPointsZh: 胸部是呼吸和循环关键器官所在区域，因此 thoracic trauma 风险高。
胸痛、呼吸困难、work of breathing、呼吸音变化都可能提示严重胸伤。
胸部创伤常常不只是骨折问题，而是气体交换与循环问题。
胸部 injuries 可能在短时间内明显恶化。
assessment 要持续回到 airway、breathing、circulation 这三条主线。
keyPointsEn: The thorax contains key structures for breathing and circulation, so thoracic trauma carries high risk.
Chest pain, dyspnea, work of breathing, and breath-sound changes may all signal major thoracic injury.
Thoracic trauma is often not just a bone problem, but a gas-exchange and circulatory problem.
Chest injuries may worsen significantly over a short time.
Assessment must repeatedly return to the airway, breathing, and circulation framework.
mustKnowZh: 要把胸部损伤看成高优先级 trauma。
要知道 thoracic injury 的危险来自呼吸和循环双重影响。
要记住 reassessment 很关键，因为病情可能快速变。
mustKnowEn: Treat chest injury as high-priority trauma.
Know that thoracic injury is dangerous because it affects both respiration and circulation.
Remember that reassessment matters because deterioration may be rapid.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把胸部创伤只当疼痛问题。实际上它可能很快影响氧合与灌注。
常见误区 2：只看伤口或瘀青，不看呼吸表现。
常见误区 3：觉得病人现在还能说话就说明胸部问题不严重。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating thoracic trauma as a pain problem only. In reality, it may rapidly affect oxygenation and perfusion.
Common confusion 2: focusing only on bruising or wounds and not on respiratory presentation.
Common confusion 3: assuming that if the patient can still talk, the chest injury is not serious.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 abdominal injuries，重点在腹部创伤外表可能不明显，但内部损伤和出血可能非常严重。它真正训练的是：看到“看起来还行”的病人时，也要持续怀疑 hidden injury。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers abdominal injuries, emphasizing that abdominal trauma may look subtle externally while internal injury and bleeding may be severe. At its core, it trains you to maintain suspicion for hidden injury even when the patient looks relatively okay.
highlightsZh: 腹部创伤容易低估
要警惕内出血与持续恶化
reassessment 在这一章特别重要
highlightsEn: Abdominal trauma is easy to underestimate
Watch for internal bleeding and ongoing deterioration
Reassessment is especially important in this chapter
keyPointsZh: 腹部创伤的危险常常藏在体表之下。
病人外观看起来不重，不代表内部损伤轻。
腹部 complaint 要和 perfusion、pain pattern、mechanism 一起看。
腹部病情常随时间进展，因此 trends 很关键。
这章训练的是“不能被表面骗过去”的临床警觉。
keyPointsEn: The danger of abdominal trauma often lies beneath the surface.
A patient who does not look dramatic externally may still have serious internal injury.
Abdominal complaints must be interpreted together with perfusion, pain pattern, and mechanism.
Abdominal conditions often evolve over time, so trends matter.
This chapter trains the kind of clinical vigilance that is not fooled by surface appearance.
mustKnowZh: 要把 abdominal injury 和 hidden bleeding 绑在一起想。
要记住 repeated assessment 的价值。
要知道“看起来不夸张”并不等于“可以放心”。
mustKnowEn: Link abdominal injury with the possibility of hidden bleeding.
Remember the value of repeated assessment.
Know that “not dramatic-looking” does not mean “safe.”
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：外表没明显伤口就低估腹部创伤。
常见误区 2：只根据当前状态判断，不看机制和趋势。
常见误区 3：觉得 abdominal pain 只是疼痛问题，忽略 perfusion risk。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: underestimating abdominal trauma because there is no obvious external wound.
Common confusion 2: judging only the current appearance without considering mechanism and trend.
Common confusion 3: treating abdominal pain as only a pain problem and ignoring perfusion risk.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 musculoskeletal injuries，重点在骨、关节、韧带、肌腱和相关血管神经问题。它真正训练的是：不要把肌骨伤只当疼痛问题，而要看功能、alignment、circulation 和 neuro status。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers musculoskeletal injuries, focusing on bones, joints, ligaments, tendons, and related vascular/neurologic issues. At its core, it teaches you not to reduce musculoskeletal injury to pain alone, but to evaluate function, alignment, circulation, and neurologic status.
highlightsZh: musculoskeletal injury 不能只看痛不痛
固定的目标是减少进一步伤害
distal pulse / sensation / function 都重要
highlightsEn: Musculoskeletal injury cannot be judged by pain alone
The goal of immobilization is to reduce further harm
Distal pulse, sensation, and function all matter
keyPointsZh: 肌骨伤 assessment 要看 pain、deformity、range of motion、circulation、sensation 和 function。
splinting / immobilization 的目标是减少 movement-related damage 和 pain。
血管神经受累会显著改变严重度判断。
某些 musculoskeletal injuries 可能是 limb-threatening，而不是“普通扭伤”。
这章要求把 anatomy knowledge 和 practical handling 结合起来。
keyPointsEn: Musculoskeletal assessment should include pain, deformity, range of motion, circulation, sensation, and function.
Splinting and immobilization aim to reduce movement-related damage and pain.
Vascular or neurologic involvement can significantly change the severity assessment.
Some musculoskeletal injuries are limb-threatening, not just “simple sprains.”
This chapter requires combining anatomic understanding with practical handling.
mustKnowZh: 不要把骨折/脱位只当疼痛问题。
远端循环与感觉检查必须是常规思维。
固定不是形式动作，而是功能性保护。
mustKnowEn: Do not treat fractures or dislocations as pain problems only.
Distal circulation and sensation checks must be routine thinking.
Immobilization is not a ritual maneuver but functional protection.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：只看 deformity，不看 neurovascular status。
常见误区 2：觉得只要固定了就不需要再评估。
常见误区 3：把肌骨伤默认成低风险。实际上某些会直接威胁肢体功能。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: focusing on deformity and forgetting neurovascular status.
Common confusion 2: assuming that after splinting, reassessment is no longer needed.
Common confusion 3: assuming musculoskeletal injuries are automatically low risk. Some directly threaten limb function.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 respiratory emergencies，是 medical 部分的最高优先级主线之一。核心在于病人是否能有效氧合和通气，以及他会不会很快因为疲劳而失代偿。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers respiratory emergencies, one of the highest-priority pathways in the medical section. Its core is whether the patient can oxygenate and ventilate effectively, and whether they are at risk of rapid decompensation from fatigue.
highlightsZh: SpO2 不是全部
work of breathing 和 fatigue 是高价值线索
呼吸病人可能很快恶化
highlightsEn: SpO2 is not the whole story
Work of breathing and fatigue are high-value clues
Respiratory patients may deteriorate quickly
keyPointsZh: 呼吸急症评估不能只看一个数字，而要看 overall respiratory picture。
病人的说话能力、姿势、呼吸工作量和疲劳程度都很重要。
氧合问题和通气问题相关但不同。
病人即使现在还能维持，也可能正在走向 respiratory failure。
这一章要求你持续 reassess，而不是一次判断就下结论。
keyPointsEn: Respiratory-emergency assessment cannot rely on one number; it must consider the overall respiratory picture.
Speech, posture, work of breathing, and fatigue are all important clues.
Oxygenation and ventilation are related but not identical problems.
A patient who is maintaining now may still be moving toward respiratory failure.
This chapter requires continuous reassessment rather than a one-time judgment.
mustKnowZh: 要把 fatigue 当成 warning sign。
要知道正常 SpO2 不等于安全。
要把 airway、breathing、ventilation 一起想。
mustKnowEn: Treat fatigue as a warning sign.
Know that a normal SpO2 does not automatically equal safety.
Think about airway, breathing, and ventilation together.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：只看 pulse ox。实际上很多危险先出现在呼吸工作量上。
常见误区 2：病人还能说话就低估病情。
常见误区 3：把呼吸病人评估成单点数据题，而不是整体临床图像。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: relying only on the pulse oximeter. Many dangers appear first in work of breathing.
Common confusion 2: underestimating severity because the patient can still talk.
Common confusion 3: treating respiratory assessment as a single-number problem instead of a whole clinical picture.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 cardiovascular emergencies，核心在于循环系统是否还能维持有效灌注。重点不仅是 chest pain，还包括 perfusion、skin signs、hemodynamic change 和心肺一体化思维。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers cardiovascular emergencies, focusing on whether the circulatory system can still maintain effective perfusion. The concern is not only chest pain, but also perfusion, skin signs, hemodynamic change, and integrated cardiopulmonary thinking.
highlightsZh: 胸痛不能只按疼痛看
skin signs 是重要 circulatory clue
心肺问题常要一起看
highlightsEn: Chest pain cannot be treated as pain alone
Skin signs are important circulatory clues
Cardiac and respiratory problems often must be assessed together
keyPointsZh: 心血管急症的核心问题是 oxygen delivery 和 organ perfusion 是否受损。
胸痛可能是 life threat，不只是疼痛 complaint。
skin color、temperature、moisture 和 mentation 都可能提供 perfusion 线索。
心脏问题和呼吸问题常常互相影响。
这章要求你把 signs、risk、perfusion 和 transport priority 一起想。
keyPointsEn: The core problem in cardiovascular emergencies is whether oxygen delivery and organ perfusion are compromised.
Chest pain may represent a life threat, not just a pain complaint.
Skin color, temperature, moisture, and mentation may all provide perfusion clues.
Cardiac and respiratory problems often affect each other.
This chapter requires integrated thinking about signs, risk, perfusion, and transport priority.
mustKnowZh: 要把 perfusion 当成核心思维。
要知道胸痛病人可能真正危险在心血管系统，而不是疼痛本身。
要记住 skin signs 也是 monitor 之外的重要数据。
mustKnowEn: Treat perfusion as a core mental model.
Know that the real danger in chest-pain patients may be the cardiovascular system, not the pain itself.
Remember that skin signs are important data beyond the monitor.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：把 chest pain 病人当止痛问题。
常见误区 2：只看 ECG 或一个数值，不看整体灌注状态。
常见误区 3：把心脏问题和呼吸问题分开想，忽略两者常常同时失衡。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: treating chest-pain patients as pain-control problems.
Common confusion 2: focusing only on ECG or one number while ignoring overall perfusion.
Common confusion 3: separating cardiac and respiratory problems when they often decompensate together.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 neurologic emergencies，重点在意识、运动、感觉、行为变化和神经系统急症的 time-sensitive 特征。它真正训练的是：不要只问“是不是中风/癫痫”，而要先看神经功能是不是正在失去。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers neurologic emergencies, focusing on consciousness, movement, sensation, behavior change, and the time-sensitive nature of neurologic crises. At its core, it teaches you not to ask only “is this stroke or seizure?” but first to determine whether neurologic function is being lost.
highlightsZh: neuro assessment 重点在 baseline change
神经问题常常是 time-sensitive
repeated neurologic reassessment 很重要
highlightsEn: Neuro assessment focuses on change from baseline
Neurologic problems are often time-sensitive
Repeated neurologic reassessment is essential
keyPointsZh: 神经急症 assessment 的核心是看功能有没有改变，而不是只看有没有 diagnosis name。
baseline 与现在的差异往往是最有价值的线索之一。
意识、运动、感觉、语言和行为变化都可能是 neurologic clue。
神经状态具有动态性，trend 比单次观察更重要。
很多 neurologic emergency 之所以高风险，是因为它们对 airway、safety、function 和 time to treatment 都有影响。
keyPointsEn: The core of neurologic-emergency assessment is recognizing loss or change in function, not simply attaching a diagnostic label.
The difference between baseline and current state is often one of the most valuable clues.
Changes in consciousness, movement, sensation, language, and behavior may all be neurologic clues.
Neurologic status is dynamic, and trend is often more important than a single observation.
Many neurologic emergencies are high risk because they affect airway, safety, function, and time to treatment.
mustKnowZh: 要先问“神经功能有没有变”，再问“它叫什么病”。
要记住 baseline comparison 很关键。
要知道 neuro patient 很需要 reassessment。
mustKnowEn: Ask first, “Has neurologic function changed?” before asking, “What is the diagnosis?”
Remember that baseline comparison is critical.
Know that neurologic patients require reassessment.
commonConfusionsZh: 常见误区 1：只在意 diagnosis 名称，不看 function 是否下降。
常见误区 2：觉得一次 neuro check 就够了。
常见误区 3：把行为异常都当精神问题，忽略 neurologic cause。
commonConfusionsEn: Common confusion 1: focusing on the diagnosis label while ignoring loss of function.
Common confusion 2: assuming one neurologic check is enough.
Common confusion 3: treating abnormal behavior as purely psychiatric while missing a neurologic cause.

reviews.summaryZh: 这章讲 endocrine emergencies，重点在代谢和内分泌失衡如何通过非特异症状表现出来。它真正训练的是：面对“看起来哪都不太对”的病人时，别忘了 endocrine 可能性。
reviews.summaryEn: This chapter covers endocrine emergencies, focusing on how metabolic and endocrine imbalance can present through nonspecific symptoms. At its core, it teaches you not to forget endocrine causes when a patient simply looks “generally unwell.”
highlightsZh: endocrine emergency 常表现不典型
history 和 objective data 都很重要
不能只看表面症状
highlightsEn: Endocrine emergencies often present atypically
Both history and objective data are important
You cannot rely on surface symptoms alone
keyPointsZh: 内分泌急症往往不是“看起来很典型”的问题，而是被 general weakness、altered LOC、nausea、odd behavior 等伪装。
血糖等 objective measure 经常是高价值线索。
既往病史、药物、baseline 和近期变化都会改变 interpretation。
endocrine problem 常常不是局部问题，而是系统性代谢失衡。
assessment 要把 symptom、history、numbers 和 overall appearance 组合起来。
keyPointsEn: Endocrine emergencies are often not dramatic textbook presentations—they may be disguised as weakness, altered LOC, nausea, or odd behavior.
Objective measures such as glucose are often high-value clues.
